title: Research Brief — Cintas Corporation's Acquisition of UniFirst Corporation
subtitle: Stage-3 grounded research brief — all load-bearing facts cite verified, source-checked claims. Analytical judgments are deferred to the IC memo.

sections.heading: Executive summary
sections.body:
On March 10, 2026, Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ: CTAS) signed a definitive agreement to acquire UniFirst Corporation (NYSE: UNF) for **$310.00 per share in cash and stock**, an enterprise value of approximately **$5.5 billion** [[deal_rationale_synergies_1]]. Each UniFirst share converts into **$155.00 in cash plus 0.7720 of a Cintas share** [[deal_rationale_synergies_2]], leaving former UniFirst holders with roughly **3.4%** of the combined company [[deal_rationale_synergies_3]][[financing_15]]. UniFirst shareholders approved the merger on June 12, 2026 [[litigation_11]]; the parties continue to target a close in the second half of calendar 2026 [[antitrust_6]].

This brings together the **#1 and a leading-but-smaller player** in North American uniform rental and facility services. Cintas is the clear scale leader — c. $10.34 billion of FY2025 revenue at a c. 22.8% operating margin [[commercial_market_2]][[commercial_market_4]] — while UniFirst earned c. $184.5 million of operating income on c. $2.43 billion of revenue, a c. 7.6% margin, roughly a third of Cintas's [[target_fundamentals_5]][[target_fundamentals_4]][[target_fundamentals_6]].

Three threads dominate the file and frame the work for the IC memo:
- **Price and synergies.** The $310 price is a c. 102.5% premium to the proxy's stated unaffected price of $153.05 [[deal_rationale_synergies_6]] and sits **above the high end of every standalone valuation methodology** produced by both financial advisors [[deal_rationale_synergies_19]]. Cintas asserts c. $375 million of run-rate cost synergies [[deal_rationale_synergies_7]] — roughly **double UniFirst's entire FY2025 operating income** [[deal_rationale_synergies_21]] — and its headline 8.0x EV/EBITDA multiple is struck **including** those synergies [[deal_rationale_synergies_8]][[deal_rationale_synergies_22]].
- **Antitrust.** The combination has drawn an **FTC Second Request**, issued at the expiry of the refiled HSR waiting period on June 11, 2026 [[antitrust_4]], after a "pull-and-refile" [[antitrust_3]]. It is characterized in trade analysis as a 3-to-2 combination of the historical "Big Three" with combined share estimated at 45–50% [[antitrust_10]][[antitrust_11]].
- **Governance and the activist.** UniFirst is family-controlled through a dual-class structure giving the Croatti family over two-thirds of the vote [[management_governance_3]]. The board rejected a $275 all-cash Cintas bid in January 2025 [[management_governance_19]], then faced an Engine Capital proxy campaign [[management_governance_9]] before agreeing to the higher cash-and-stock deal in February 2026 [[management_governance_21]].

sections.heading: The transaction
sections.body:
The headline terms, as grounded in the deal press release, the merger 8-K, the S-4 and the DEFM14A:

| Term | Detail | |
|---|---|---|
| Consideration | $155.00 cash + 0.7720 Cintas share per UniFirst share | [[deal_rationale_synergies_2]] |
| Implied value | c. $310.00/share (Cintas $200.77 close, Mar 9 2026) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_4]] |
| Enterprise value | c. $5.5 billion | [[deal_rationale_synergies_1]] |
| Implied multiple | 8.0x run-rate TTM EBITDA, **including** c. $375M synergies | [[deal_rationale_synergies_8]] |
| Stated cost synergies | c. $375 million run-rate (Cintas's assertion) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_7]] |
| Unaffected price | $153.05 (Nov 11 2025) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_5]] |
| Implied premium | c. 102.5% to unaffected price | [[deal_rationale_synergies_6]] |
| Pro-forma ownership | c. 96.6% Cintas / c. 3.4% former UniFirst | [[financing_15]] |
| Stock issued | c. 14.26 million Cintas shares | [[deal_rationale_synergies_3]] |
| Net leverage at close | c. 1.5x debt/EBITDA (Cintas's assertion) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_10]] |
| EPS accretion | by end of the second full year after close (Cintas's assertion) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_9]] |
| Target / reverse termination fee | $213.3M (UniFirst) / $350M (Cintas) | [[merger_agreement_2]] |
| Shareholder vote | approved June 12, 2026 | [[litigation_11]] |
| Regulatory status | FTC Second Request issued June 11, 2026 | [[antitrust_4]] |
| Target close | second half of calendar 2026 | [[antitrust_6]] |

The combined company would serve approximately 1.5 million business customers across North America [[commercial_market_27]].

sections.heading: The companies
sections.body:
**UniFirst Corporation (the target).** UniFirst is one of the leading providers of workplace uniforms and protective clothing in North America, serving over 300,000 customer locations across the U.S., Canada and Europe [[target_fundamentals_1]], with approximately 16,000 "team partners" [[target_fundamentals_2]]. It is **vertically integrated**, manufacturing approximately 62% of the garments it places in service [[target_fundamentals_3]]. Revenue is overwhelmingly route-based and recurring, under written service contracts of three-to-five-year duration [[target_fundamentals_20]]. FY2025 revenue was essentially flat at c. $2,432.4 million [[target_fundamentals_4]] and operating income was c. $184.5 million, up 0.5% [[target_fundamentals_5]] — a c. 7.6% operating margin [[target_fundamentals_6]]. The company generated c. $296.9 million of operating cash flow [[target_fundamentals_16]] against c. $154.3 million of capex [[target_fundamentals_17]] — capital intensity of roughly 52% of operating cash flow [[target_fundamentals_18]]. Its stated growth strategy leans on continued acquisitions [[target_fundamentals_21]], and it is mid-stream on CRM/ERP "Key Initiatives" [[target_fundamentals_22]]; management says these investments are beginning to show returns [[target_fundamentals_23]].

UniFirst's segment revenue mix (FY2025):

| Segment | Revenue | % of total | |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform & Facility Service Solutions | c. $2,218.6M | c. 91.2% | [[target_fundamentals_9]][[target_fundamentals_10]] |
| First Aid & Safety Solutions | c. $114.6M | c. 4.7% | [[target_fundamentals_11]][[target_fundamentals_12]] |
| Other | c. $99.2M | c. 4.1% | [[target_fundamentals_13]] |

By geography, c. $2,245.9M is U.S. with c. $186.5M from Europe and Canada [[target_fundamentals_14]]; c. 7.0% of revenue is non-USD [[target_fundamentals_15]]. Normalized for FY2024's extra week, Uniform & Facility organic growth was c. +1.7% and First Aid grew c. +10.0% [[target_fundamentals_7]][[target_fundamentals_8]].

**Cintas Corporation (the acquirer).** Cintas describes itself as North America's leading provider of corporate identity uniforms through rental and sales programs [[commercial_market_5]], reporting through Uniform Rental & Facility Services and First Aid & Safety Services segments [[commercial_market_1]]. FY2025 (ended May 31, 2025) revenue was c. $10,340.2 million, up 7.7% [[commercial_market_2]], with operating income of c. $2.36 billion — a c. 22.8% margin [[commercial_market_4]]. It employs approximately 48,300 [[commercial_market_6]] and characterizes its diverse customer base such that no single customer is more than 1% of revenue [[commercial_market_8]]. On scale, Cintas's c. $10.34 billion of revenue is roughly 4.3x UniFirst's and 3.8x Vestis's [[commercial_market_29]].

sections.heading: Industry & business model
sections.body:
Uniform rental / facility services is a **route-based, recurring-revenue** business: garments and facility products are rented, laundered and delivered weekly from processing plants and branches over fixed local delivery routes. Both Cintas and UniFirst describe the markets as **local in nature and highly fragmented** [[commercial_market_7]], with low customer concentration on all sides [[commercial_market_8]][[commercial_market_18]].

Structurally, the U.S./Canada market is a **three-national-player oligopoly over a fragmented local-route base** — the public participants being Cintas, UniFirst and Vestis, alongside privately held Alsco and a long regional tail [[commercial_market_28]][[commercial_market_9]]. The competitive landscape was reshaped by the 2023 carve-out of **Vestis** from Aramark (Distribution Date September 30, 2023) [[commercial_market_11]]; Vestis calls itself the #2 provider in the U.S./Canada [[commercial_market_13]]. Vestis has since deteriorated: FY2025 revenue of c. $2.7 billion at only a 2.4% operating margin and a **net loss of $40.2 million** (a 291.8% swing from prior-year profit) [[commercial_market_14]][[commercial_market_15]], driven by rental revenue declines where lost business exceeded new business [[commercial_market_16]][[commercial_market_17]]. That a weakened #2 emerged just as Cintas moved on the #3-scale player materially shapes the "why now" [[commercial_market_30]].

**Comparable consolidations (precedent M&A).** Sector M&A is the natural backdrop to this deal:

| Deal | Year | Terms / value | Note | |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cintas / G&K Services | 2016 | $97.50/share cash, c. $2.2B EV | c. $130–140M synergies expected | [[commercial_market_22]][[commercial_market_23]] |
| Aramark / AmeriPride | 2017 | (uniform/linen rental) | a financial-advisor precedent comp | [[commercial_market_20]] |
| Aramark → Vestis spin-off | 2023 | carve-out | created the standalone #2 | [[commercial_market_11]] |
| Cintas → UniFirst (rebuffed) | 2022 | $255/share indication | rejected | [[commercial_market_25]] |
| Cintas → UniFirst (rebuffed) | Jan 2025 | $275/share cash, c. $5.3B, c. 46% premium | rejected | [[commercial_market_24]] |
| Cintas → UniFirst (signed) | Mar 2026 | $155 cash + 0.7720 share | the deal under review | [[commercial_market_26]] |

sections.heading: Deal rationale & synergies — valuation and the price
sections.body:
The $310 price is rich against every independent yardstick in the record. The two financial advisors' standalone methodologies, and where the $310 lands:

| Methodology | Reference range (per share unless noted) | |
|---|---|---|
| J.P. Morgan — public trading multiples | 8.0x–10.0x EV/2026E adj. EBITDA | [[deal_rationale_synergies_11]] |
| J.P. Morgan — selected transactions | 11.7x–14.2x FV/LTM EBITDA | [[deal_rationale_synergies_12]] |
| J.P. Morgan — DCF | implied per-share equity range (7.50%–8.50% discount) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_13]] |
| Goldman Sachs — DCF | 9.0% discount rate (CAPM cost of equity) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_14]] |
| Goldman Sachs — selected precedents | 11.7x+ EV/LTM EBITDA (deals since 2015) | [[deal_rationale_synergies_15]][[deal_rationale_synergies_16]] |
| Goldman Sachs — premia paid (568 deals) | 21%–59% premium applied to $153.05 | [[deal_rationale_synergies_17]][[deal_rationale_synergies_18]] |

The comparable-company set used was Vestis and Elis SA [[deal_rationale_synergies_25]]. Two grounded observations distilled from these anchors: the **$310 sits above the high end of every standalone methodology** from both banks [[deal_rationale_synergies_19]], and the **c. 102.5% headline premium is nearly triple the 36% median** of Goldman's 568-deal premia-paid sample (102.5 / 36 ≈ 2.85x) [[deal_rationale_synergies_20]].

**On synergies:** Cintas asserts c. $375 million of run-rate operating cost synergies — spanning material cost, production, service and SG&A [[deal_rationale_synergies_7]]. Two sizing facts frame their credibility: that figure is c. 2.03x UniFirst's entire FY2025 operating income [[deal_rationale_synergies_21]], and the headline 8.0x EV/EBITDA multiple is computed **including** the synergies — backing them out raises the effective ex-synergy multiple materially [[deal_rationale_synergies_22]]. Cintas states the deal is accretive to EPS by the end of the second full year [[deal_rationale_synergies_9]]. (Synergy size, accretion and "strategic fit" are the acquirer's own assertions and are scoped as such in the claims.)

sections.heading: Financing & balance sheet
sections.body:
The cash leg is funded from cash on hand and committed credit, with **no financing condition** to closing [[financing_1]][[merger_agreement_4]]. Cintas secured fully committed bridge financing from Morgan Stanley, KeyBank and Wells Fargo [[financing_5]] under a March 10, 2026 commitment letter [[financing_10]]; after Tranche B was replaced by a new revolver, the remaining bridge commitment is $1.6 billion [[financing_11]]. On March 27, 2026 Cintas entered a **$2.0 billion revolving credit facility** [[financing_7]] carrying a leverage covenant (consolidated indebtedness / EBITDA) [[financing_8]] and pricing of SOFR + 70–114 bps [[financing_9]]. Before closing, Cintas expects to execute **c. $2.8 billion of permanent debt financing** [[financing_12]], at an assumed 5% rate [[financing_14]], taking on roughly $2.8 billion of additional indebtedness [[financing_13]].

Cintas states net leverage at close will be c. 1.5x debt/EBITDA [[financing_2]]. Three grounded reads on the structure: it is **structured conservatively for an investment-grade acquirer**, with the new debt sitting well inside the revolver's covenant headroom [[financing_17]]; **accretion is back-end-loaded and synergy-dependent**, expected only by end of year two after c. $2.8 billion of new debt [[financing_18]]; and **financing/liquidity close-risk is low but not zero** — the cash leg is not subject to a financing condition and is bridge-backstopped, but permanent financing was not yet secured at the proxy date [[financing_19]][[financing_16]].

sections.heading: Merger agreement & deal protections
sections.body:
The agreement's risk-allocation terms (from the DEFM14A summary, the agreement annex and the merger 8-K):

| Provision | Term | |
|---|---|---|
| Target termination fee | $213.3M (UniFirst → Cintas) | [[merger_agreement_1]] |
| Reverse termination fee | $350M (Cintas → UniFirst) | [[merger_agreement_2]] |
| Fee cap | payable on no more than one occasion | [[merger_agreement_3]] |
| Financing condition | none — closing not conditioned on financing | [[merger_agreement_4]] |
| Specific performance | available; parties will not oppose | [[merger_agreement_14]] |
| Regulatory conditions | HSR clearance + a foreign regulator + UniFirst permit consents | [[merger_agreement_6]][[merger_agreement_7]] |
| Other conditions | UniFirst shareholder approval; NASDAQ listing of Cintas shares | [[merger_agreement_8]][[merger_agreement_9]] |
| MAC/MAE | both Company-side and Parent-side bring-down reps | [[merger_agreement_10]][[merger_agreement_11]] |
| Deal protection | no-shop with keep-informed on a superior proposal | [[merger_agreement_12]][[merger_agreement_13]] |
| Outside date | termination right if not closed by a stated outside date | [[merger_agreement_19]] |

The negotiation record is unusually informative on antitrust risk-sharing: UniFirst's advisor sought an "above market" reverse fee and a **"hell or high water"** regulatory-efforts covenant [[merger_agreement_15]]; Cintas at one point terminated discussions over that covenant, the fee, and a "high 300s" price [[merger_agreement_16]]; the parties settled on a **$350M reverse fee plus an obligation to litigate antitrust challenges** [[merger_agreement_17]], with two four-month extensions available on a 10-month outside date [[merger_agreement_18]]. The grounded synthesis: the agreement **shifts a defined slice of antitrust risk to Cintas but stops short of an unlimited hell-or-high-water commitment** [[merger_agreement_20]], and because Cintas's closing obligation is not financing-conditioned yet specifically enforceable, Cintas bears financing-availability risk [[merger_agreement_21]].

sections.heading: Antitrust & regulatory — the dominant close-risk
sections.body:
The HSR timeline is the spine of the regulatory story: both parties filed on April 8, 2026 [[antitrust_2]]; Cintas executed a **"pull-and-refile"**, withdrawing effective May 8 and refiling [[antitrust_3]]; and at the refiled waiting period's expiry on **June 11, 2026 the FTC issued a Second Request** [[antitrust_4]]. Closing also requires a foreign antitrust clearance and certain permit consents [[antitrust_5]]. As of its June 12 approval, the company still expected an H2-2026 close [[antitrust_6]].

On concentration: UniFirst's own 10-K names Cintas, Alsco and Vestis as its principal national competitors [[antitrust_1]]. Trade analysis (labeled as such, not primary) estimates the combined entity at **45–50% of the North American market** [[antitrust_10]] and frames the deal as a 3-to-2 combination of the "Big Three" [[antitrust_11]]; Dealreporter reports the FTC interviewing competitors [[antitrust_7]] and notes that finding divestiture buyers may be hard [[antitrust_8]] given that Vestis — the natural national buyer — was downgraded by S&P to 'B' in December 2025 [[antitrust_9]].

On the **base rate**, two directly comparable route-based deals cleared without structural relief: Cintas/G&K Services (2017) [[antitrust_12]] and Aramark/AmeriPride, which received HSR early termination [[antitrust_13]]. The grounded reads: the base rate for clearing such deals is favorable [[antitrust_17]], but the realistic remedy path here skews toward **localized route/branch divestitures rather than a block — with elevated execution risk** precisely because the strongest national divestiture buyer is impaired [[antitrust_18]].

sections.heading: Management, governance & the activist
sections.body:
UniFirst is **family-controlled**: each Class B share carries 10 votes [[management_governance_2]], giving the Croatti family over two-thirds of combined voting power [[management_governance_3]]. Because the merger needs a two-thirds vote [[management_governance_4]] and the family signed a voting-and-support agreement [[management_governance_5]], approval was effectively assured from signing [[management_governance_6]]. Governance is tightly held: Cynthia Croatti is the only family member on the board, and the General Counsel — who participated in board meetings — is her stepson [[management_governance_7]]; CEO Steven Sintros personally led the Cintas contact [[management_governance_8]].

**The chronology of the "flip."** Cintas first approached at $255/share in February 2022 [[commercial_market_25]]; the board publicly rejected a **$275/share all-cash** bid on January 7, 2025 [[management_governance_19]]. **Engine Capital** (c. 3.2% of common) [[management_governance_13]] then ran a proxy campaign asserting deeply disappointing total shareholder returns [[management_governance_9]], an operating-margin decline from 12.8% (2019) to an expected 6.4% (FY2026) [[management_governance_10]], and that rejecting $275 squandered value [[management_governance_11]]; it nominated two directors [[management_governance_14]] and won ISS support [[management_governance_15]]. The board recommended against Engine [[management_governance_16]] and **defeated its slate** at the December 15, 2025 meeting, keeping control intact [[management_governance_18]]. Yet on February 8, 2026 the board agreed to **$310/share** [[management_governance_21]] in a cash-and-stock structure [[management_governance_22]], versus the earlier all-cash offers. The grounded read: the flip is best explained by **price and structure, not loss of control** [[management_governance_23]] — though vote certainty rests on a related-party / interested-controller dynamic [[management_governance_24]].

**The bear case (Engine Capital, an interested party).** Engine argues that since founder-CEO Ron Croatti's 2017 death UniFirst has materially underperformed [[short_activist_1]] on poor operational execution and capital allocation [[short_activist_2]], that years of heavy investment have not paid off [[short_activist_3]], and that the competitive gap with Cintas keeps widening [[short_activist_4]]. All three leading proxy advisors (ISS, Glass Lewis, Egan-Jones) backed Engine's slate [[short_activist_10]]. Notably, this is an **activist proxy contest, not a short-seller report** [[short_activist_11]] — no dedicated short thesis on UNF or CTAS was located [[short_activist_12]].

sections.heading: Operational profile & integration
sections.body:
This is operationally a **large route-network bolt-on**, not a merger of equals: Cintas runs c. 12,100 local delivery routes from 478 facilities with c. 22,900 vehicles [[operational_integration_1]][[operational_integration_2]], versus UniFirst's 281 facilities (c. 8.3M sq ft) [[operational_integration_5]], a c. 4,813-vehicle fleet (about one-fifth of Cintas's) [[operational_integration_7]], and c. 16,000 employees (about a third of Cintas's) [[operational_integration_6]][[operational_integration_24]]. Both are plant-and-branch route models [[operational_integration_3]]; route servicing is c. 83.8% of UniFirst's revenue, under 3-to-5-year contracts, so customer attrition during integration is a live risk [[operational_integration_8]][[operational_integration_9]].

The synergy mechanism Cintas asserts runs through combining processing capacity, route networks and supply chains and optimizing shared infrastructure [[operational_integration_16]][[operational_integration_17]][[operational_integration_25]]. Three grounded integration-risk facts temper it: the two firms run **structurally different supply chains** (UniFirst self-manufactures c. 62% of garments; Cintas largely outsources and runs five plants) [[operational_integration_10]][[operational_integration_11]][[operational_integration_27]]; UniFirst entered the deal with an **unremediated IT material weakness** narrowed to its CRM [[operational_integration_12]][[operational_integration_13]] and a multi-year ERP program running to 2027 with $45.3M capitalized [[operational_integration_14]][[operational_integration_15]]; and the proxy itself flags integration execution risk [[operational_integration_19]], non-recurring integration costs [[operational_integration_20]], and key-person/workforce retention risk [[operational_integration_21]].

sections.heading: Litigation & legal
sections.body: Litigation is a **clean, low tail-risk** for this deal. Both companies disclose only ordinary-course claims [[litigation_1]][[litigation_6]], and Cintas management opines its ordinary-course exposure is not material [[litigation_7]]. As of the DEFM14A date (May 11, 2026) there were **no lawsuits challenging the merger** and no securities-class or derivative suits filed [[litigation_8]][[litigation_9]], though the proxy notes such suits are commonly brought [[litigation_10]]; the shareholder vote then cleared on June 12, 2026 [[litigation_11]]. Two non-merger watch-items remain: UniFirst's unresolved **Mexican tax assessment**, still on appeal [[litigation_3]][[litigation_4]], and a Cintas reference to litigation "not considered in the ordinary course" whose size is not disclosed in the primaries [[litigation_5]]. The grounded read: litigation is low-risk with two contingent, non-merger watch-items [[litigation_12]][[litigation_13]].

sections.heading: Supply chain & vertical integration
sections.body:
UniFirst is unusually **vertically integrated** for the sector: it self-manufactures c. 62% of the garments it places in service [[supplychain_1]] from its own plants (San Luis Potosí, Mexico and Managua, Nicaragua, plus subcontractors) [[supplychain_3]] and produces c. 99% of its floor mats at Cave City, Arkansas [[supplychain_4]], citing better control of quality, price and speed for custom programs [[supplychain_2]]. Cintas runs the **opposite model** — it sources finished products from many outside suppliers [[supplychain_10]], operating only five of its own manufacturing facilities [[supplychain_11]] alongside 12 distribution centers [[supplychain_13]].

What the combination means for Cintas: it would acquire **in-house manufacturing capacity and a captive supply chain it has historically chosen not to own** [[supplychain_16]] — but also inherit UniFirst's Mexico/Nicaragua manufacturing footprint and its associated **tariff and imported-input exposure** [[supplychain_17]][[supplychain_6]][[supplychain_7]], a sensitivity Cintas already flags through trade-agreement / geopolitical risk [[supplychain_15]]. Both carry merchandise-in-service amortization (UniFirst over 6–36 months) as a real cost line [[supplychain_9]][[supplychain_14]]. Whether captive manufacturing is a strategic asset or an integration burden for Cintas's outsourced model is a judgment for the memo.

sections.heading: How the cost synergies get realized — and whether the business supports it
sections.body:
The c. $375M is a **cost** figure, and UniFirst's own cost structure tells you where it must come from. UniFirst's cost of revenues was c. $1,542.4M — **63.4% of revenue** [[synmech_1]] — with merchandise amortization and production payroll as named components [[synmech_3]], plus SG&A of c. $565.1M (c. 23.2%) [[synmech_5]]; it runs a c. 4,813-vehicle delivery fleet [[synmech_6]]. Because the cost base is dominated by **route delivery, processing-plant labor, merchandise and overhead**, the realizable levers are the classic route-density ones — Cintas frames the synergy as "integrating complementary processing capacity, route networks, service infrastructure, supply chains" [[synmech_10]] and leveraging the combined route networks [[synmech_12]]: overlapping routes consolidated into more stops per truck, processing plants and branches utilized harder, procurement scaled, and duplicate public-company overhead removed. Critically, the filings do **not** disclose a dollar split of route vs. plant vs. merchandise cost, so the precise mechanism is a *reasoned* judgment for the memo, not a cited figure.

**Base rate — does Cintas execute laundry-consolidation synergies?** Its 2016 G&K Services acquisition targeted c. $130–140M of synergies over four years [[synmech_13]][[synmech_14]]; integration spend wound down to c. $7.8M by FY2019 [[synmech_15]], a pattern consistent with realized synergy, though Cintas never published an explicit synergy-realization reconciliation. For scale, the $375M target is c. 2.03x UniFirst's FY2025 operating income [[deal_rationale_synergies_21]] and is embedded in the 8.0x headline multiple [[synmech_11]].

sections.heading: Why Cintas is so persistent
sections.body:
Cintas pursued UniFirst for **nearly four years across at least five distinct approaches** [[whycintas_17]]: a $255/share indication in February 2022 [[whycintas_1]], a $275 proposal in November 2024 [[whycintas_3]] reiterated repeatedly [[whycintas_4]][[whycintas_5]], made **public** in January 2025 [[whycintas_6]], formally terminated in March 2025 [[whycintas_8]], then **re-submitted** in December 2025 during the Engine Capital proxy fight [[whycintas_9]][[whycintas_10]] before the February 2026 agreement at $310 [[whycintas_11]]. Cintas frames the rationale as combining complementary route networks [[whycintas_12]] to serve c. 1.5 million customers [[whycintas_13]] and "better compete in an increasingly competitive environment" [[whycintas_14]], and characterized completing the transaction as a top priority [[whycintas_7]].

A structural fact underpins the persistence: UniFirst is one of only **three scaled national players** named in the filings (Cintas, Alsco, Vestis) [[whycintas_15]] in a highly competitive industry [[whycintas_16]] — i.e. the last sizeable, independent, family-controlled operator available to consolidate [[whycintas_18]]. Whether that makes the pursuit offensive lead-extension or defensive consolidation (before a weakened Vestis or a financial buyer takes the asset) is the memo's call.

sections.heading: Customer base: why only ~4 wearers per customer
sections.body:
The deck's penetration framing — a combined **<8M uniform "wearers" across <2M customers** (c. 4 per customer), in a 180M+ worker / 16M business market — looks oddly low, but the verified facts explain it. Cintas serves **"over one million businesses of all types, from small service and manufacturing companies to major corporations that employ thousands of people"** [[custmix_1]], and UniFirst likewise serves "businesses of all sizes across multiple industries and sectors" [[custmix_3]] — a base **dominated by small businesses by count**, each with only a handful of uniformed staff. And uniforms are only part of the offering: c. **95% of Cintas's revenue is route servicing across Uniform Rental & Facility Services, First Aid & Safety, and Fire Protection** [[custmix_2]] — so a large share of accounts buy floor mats, restroom supplies, first-aid cabinets or fire protection with **few or no rented-uniform wearers at all**. The base is therefore **broad and shallow** — millions of small, mixed-service stops rather than a few large uniformed accounts [[custmix_4]].

(The <8M / <2M figures themselves are from the image-PDF investor deck — management framing, not machine-verified.) This same structural feature is *why* route density is the core synergy lever: a business made of millions of small, dispersed stops is exactly what consolidating overlapping routes optimizes.

sections.heading: Preliminary read & coverage
sections.body:
**What the facts establish (for the IC memo to weigh).** This is a strategically coherent #1-buys-#3 route-density consolidation at a **full price**: $310 is above every standalone valuation anchor [[deal_rationale_synergies_19]] and the premium is near-triple the precedent median [[deal_rationale_synergies_20]], so the economics lean heavily on Cintas realizing c. $375M of cost synergies that are themselves c. 2x the target's operating income [[deal_rationale_synergies_21]]. Deal *certainty on the vote* is structurally high (family lock-up) [[management_governance_6]], but *certainty on closing* hinges on the **FTC Second Request** [[antitrust_4]] in a 3-to-2 market [[antitrust_11]] where the natural divestiture buyer is impaired [[antitrust_9]] — partly mitigated by the $350M reverse fee and litigate-the-challenge covenant [[merger_agreement_17]][[merger_agreement_20]]. The "good deal vs. will-it-close" questions should be argued separately.

**Coverage.** 10 of 12 checklist areas are covered with verified claims; the two not searched — macro/geopolitical and ESG/environmental — were the optional tail outside the approved scope, and the coverage map reports **0 required/recommended gaps**. No covered area was flagged thin.

**Honest limitations from the research pass:**
- **Synergy lever / cost-vs-revenue split:** the c. $375M is grounded as Cintas's assertion with its broad buckets, but UniFirst's cost composition is now grounded (cost of revenues c. 63.4% of revenue; SG&A c. 23.2%), and the Dec 2025 investor deck was read manually (it is an image-PDF, so not machine-verifiable through the spine). But the filings still do **not** disclose a dollar split of route vs. plant vs. merchandise cost, so the precise per-stop density mechanism remains a *reasoned judgment* for the memo, not a cited figure.
- **Market size / share:** no primary filing gives a U.S. TAM; the 45–50% combined-share and "~$20B market" figures are **trade-press/analyst estimates, labeled as such**, corroborated only against each filer's competitor list.
- **Exact MAE carve-outs, matching-rights day-count, and the calendar outside date** did not extract cleanly from the agreement's glossary/definitions and are residual gaps.
- **Quarantines (14 of 262 proposals):** all either duplicates of facts verified elsewhere (e.g. the $350M reverse fee / hell-or-high-water record, verified under the merger-agreement sources) or non-load-bearing details (a Cintas rental-margin datapoint, the G&K realized-synergy reconciliation); no load-bearing fact was lost.
